us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed as Iran Conflict Escalates Toward Deadline
us-politics.body:
American military aircraft have been shot down by enemy fire for the first time in over 20 years, as the US conflict with Iran intensifies. Trump has warned Tehran that 'time is running out' before 'all hell' rains down, with Senator Graham urging Iran to 'choose wisely.' The losses prompted Rep. Seth Moulton to declare that 'Iran is winning the war,' a charge that sharpens debate over US strategy.

Supporters argue the US can prevail through attrition without ground troops, while critics question what 'finishing the job' actually means in practice. Fox News analyst Tevi Troy frames Trump as shouldering the burdens of a wartime presidency. The stakes are high on the home front too - Secretary Rubio revoked the green cards of two relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani, who were subsequently arrested by ICE.

sources.title: TEVI TROY: Trump faces the burdens of a wartime presidency
US military jets hit in Iran war are the first shot down by enemy fire in over 20 years
The US can beat Iran at its own game, and without boots on the ground
ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict
Graham backs Trump’s Iran ultimatum as deadline approaches: ‘Choose wisely’
In Iran, what does ‘finish the job’ even mean?
Trump warns Iran: ‘Time is running out’ before ‘all hell’ rains down
Moulton says ‘Iran is winning the war’ after US jets downed

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Proposes Defense Surge, Deep Domestic Cuts
us-politics.body:
The White House has unveiled a budget proposing $1.5 trillion in defense spending alongside sharp reductions to domestic programs. The plan also includes a $152 million request to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison. Supporters say the shift reflects a serious reordering of national priorities at a moment of active military conflict.

Critics warn the domestic cuts will accelerate economic pain for ordinary Americans, with some analysts arguing the country is already heading toward a severe recession. A new Gallup poll adds pressure, showing China has overtaken the US in global approval ratings - a reputational slide that complicates the administration's case for American leadership abroad.

sources.title: China now tops US in global approval ratings: Gallup
Trump unveils $1.5T defense surge, deep domestic cuts — what’s on the budget chopping block
America is heading for a recession — and it may be the worst yet
Alcatraz could reopen as a 'state-of-the-art secure prison' under Trump's $152M budget request

us-politics.headline: Courts Check Trump on College Admissions and White House Ballroom
us-politics.body:
A federal judge has blocked the Trump administration's effort to require colleges to certify they do not consider race in admissions, handing the White House a legal setback on education policy. Separately, Trump filed an emergency appeal to continue construction of a ballroom inside the White House complex, after a court moved to halt the project. The two rulings in a single day illustrate how aggressively courts are scrutinising executive action.

Sen. Blumenthal dismissed Trump's earlier college sports reform order as 'theater, not a fix.' The pattern points to a broader tension between an administration pushing hard on multiple policy fronts and a judiciary increasingly willing to intervene.

sources.title: What we know about the White House ballroom bunker
Trump files emergency appeal to keep building White House ballroom
Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions
Blumenthal: Trump’s order to reform college sports ‘is theater, not a fix’

us-politics.headline: Republicans Eye Midterm Trouble as Political Landscape Shifts
us-politics.body:
Former White House press secretary Kayleigh McEnany has warned that the 2026 midterms 'will be hard' for Republicans, a rare public admission of vulnerability from within the party's orbit. In Virginia, a narrow majority now backs redistricting that would favour Democrats, signalling competitive terrain ahead. The political map is moving.

On the Democratic side, Rep. Eric Swalwell's clash with the FBI is drawing scrutiny as he eyes a California governor run, while a separate opinion piece argues the Constitution may need amending to resolve the birthright citizenship debate. Both parties are navigating contested ground heading into the next electoral cycle.

sources.title: McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Swalwell-FBI clash draws attention to Trump foe’s California governor bid
Narrow majority in Virginia backs redistricting to favor Democrats: Poll
Reclaiming our own birthright: We might need to amend the Constitution

au-politics.headline: Labor's Gambling Ad Reforms Draw Sharp Criticism From Independents
au-politics.body:
The Albanese government has released its response to the Murphy report on gambling advertising, opting for partial restrictions rather than the sweeping ban many advocates demanded. Senator David Pocock has condemned the approach as inadequate, arguing Labor chose industry interests over public health.

The government's position reflects a familiar tension - balancing pressure from broadcasters and sporting codes against growing community concern about gambling harm. Critics say the reforms amount to tinkering while addiction rates climb. Supporters argue incremental change is more durable than overreach.

With an election approaching, the decision carries political risk on both sides. A full ban would have alienated powerful media and sports lobbies. Doing too little hands ammunition to crossbenchers already positioning themselves as the genuine reform vote.

sources.title: We cannot celebrate tinkering when it comes to gambling reform. Labor’s response falls tragically short | David Pocock
Trash or treasure? Albanese's big gamble

au-politics.headline: Victoria Holds First Nations Treaty Elections as Process Advances
au-politics.body:
Victoria's First Nations treaty body has begun its inaugural elections, marking a concrete step in what remains Australia's most advanced state-level treaty process. The vote gives Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Victorians direct say in who represents them at the negotiating table.

Advocates describe the elections as a shift from consultation to genuine self-determination. The phrase 'we're the experts in our own lives' captures the mood among participants who have long argued that government-designed processes undermine the very autonomy they claim to advance.

The treaty process remains contested. Supporters see it as overdue recognition of First Nations sovereignty. Critics question the pace, the representativeness of the body, and whether state governments will ultimately deliver meaningful outcomes once negotiations begin.

sources.title: ‘We’re the experts in our own lives’: Victoria’s First Nations treaty body is holding its first elections

au-politics.headline: Stagflation Warning Hits as Fuel Costs Squeeze Young Workers
au-politics.body:
A leading economist has declared stagflation is already underway in Australia, combining sluggish growth with persistent inflation in a combination that limits the Reserve Bank's options. Rate cuts risk stoking prices further; holding rates squeezes households already under pressure.

The pain is landing unevenly. Young Australians in outer suburbs and regional areas say fuel costs have made commuting to work financially irrational, forcing some to reduce hours or quit jobs entirely. The problem is structural - low wages, high petrol prices, and limited public transport converging on the same people.

Economists disagree on remedies. Some argue targeted cost-of-living relief is essential. Others warn government spending at this point would entrench the inflation driving the crisis in the first place.

sources.title: Stagflation 'is happening' says a leading economist. What can be done about it?
'Doesn't make any sense': Young Aussies can't justify fuel costs to get to work

au-politics.headline: Australian Activists Plan Return Gaza Flotilla Amid Escalating War
au-politics.body:
A group of Australian activists is preparing to join a second flotilla attempt to reach Gaza as fighting across the region intensifies. Participants say they are aware of the risks, with one asking publicly whether they will be punished for returning.

The effort reflects sustained pressure from parts of the Australian community over the humanitarian situation in Gaza. Lebanese Australians have separately described watching in despair as Israeli bombardment forces their families to flee, with some reporting they feel entirely powerless to help relatives caught in the conflict.

The Australian government has not publicly endorsed the flotilla. The missions sit in contested legal and diplomatic territory, and previous attempts have been intercepted or turned back before reaching their destination.

sources.title: ‘Will I be punished for daring to return’: Australian flotilla activists to head back to Gaza as wider war rages
‘You feel utterly helpless’: Lebanese Australians watch in despair as Israeli bombardment forces families to flee

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates as Pilot Goes Missing
world.body:
US and Israeli forces have expanded strikes on Iranian infrastructure, hitting universities, bridges, and a psychiatric hospital in Tehran. A projectile struck near Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one person, prompting the UN's nuclear watchdog to voice deep concern. Iran's government condemned the attacks, saying the US and Israel "belong in the Stone Age."

A US fighter jet was shot down over Iran, leaving an airman missing in what analysts are calling a serious test for Washington. Trump issued a 48-hour ultimatum to Tehran to negotiate a deal while the search continues. The US separately arrested relatives of slain general Qasem Soleimani, with Secretary of State Rubio stripping Soleimani's niece of her US residency. Pakistan and European nations including Italy are scrambling to manage the war's regional fallout.

sources.title: How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?
Search for missing airman presents serious test for US
What we know so far about the US fighter jet shot down over Iran
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
Tehran psychiatric hospital not usable after US-Israeli strike
Universities hit as US, Israel ramp up attacks on Iran’s infrastructure
Iran’s coastline: architecture of control
Italy’s Meloni meets Qatar emir to discuss energy issues amid Iran war
Marco Rubio says he has stripped Qassem Soleimani’s niece of US residency
Trump says Iran has 48 hours to make deal as search for US pilot continues
UN watchdog voices 'deep concern' as Iran reports new attacks on nuclear plant
Projectile hits near Iran’s Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one: IAEA

world.headline: Israeli Strikes Hit Lebanon and Gaza as Protests Suppressed
world.body:
Israeli strikes killed two girls and damaged a hospital in the Lebanese city of Tyre. Indonesia received the bodies of UN peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon, underscoring the growing toll on international forces in the region. Displaced Lebanese civilians describe lives "turned upside down" with little prospect of return.

In Gaza, Israeli drone strikes continued, with footage showing vehicles engulfed in flames in central Gaza. Inside Israel, police arrested antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv. Three suspects in the UK were remanded in custody over an attack on a Jewish charity, a case authorities are treating as connected to broader tensions over the conflicts.

sources.title: Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon
Israeli police arrest antiwar protesters in Tel Aviv
Vehicle engulfed in flames after Israeli drone strike in central Gaza
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Three suspects ordered to stay in UK custody over Jewish charity attack
Witness records Israeli strike on building in Lebanon’s Tyre
Vehicles burn in Israel after being hit with Iranian projectile debris
Israeli attacks on southern Lebanon kill two girls, damage hospital

world.headline: Russia Kills Five in Ukraine Market Strike
world.body: A Russian attack on a market in Ukraine killed five people, the latest in a series of strikes targeting civilian areas. The attack drew renewed condemnation but no immediate shift in the diplomatic stalemate over the war.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five

world.headline: World's Oldest Leader Names First-Ever Deputy
world.body: The world's oldest serving head of state has appointed a deputy for the first time in a 43-year rule, signalling a rare and closely watched political transition. No successor has been formally designated, and the move raises questions about the future direction of the country's government.

sources.title: World's oldest leader to get a deputy for first time in 43-year rule

ai.headline: Anthropic Locks OpenClaw Out of Claude Code Subscriptions
ai.body:
Anthropic has effectively blocked OpenClaw from its Claude Code platform by requiring subscribers to pay extra for the integration - a move critics are calling a de facto ban. The decision landed hard in developer communities, with Hacker News threads filling quickly with complaints from users who built workflows around the combined tooling.

The timing is notable. Anthropic is riding high in private markets, with investor appetite strong despite competition from OpenAI and Google. Squeezing a popular third-party integration suggests the company is tightening control over its ecosystem as it scales. Meanwhile, hackers are circulating a Claude Code leak bundled with malware, adding a security headache to an already turbulent week for the company.

sources.title: Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party

ai.headline: Claude Code Finds 23-Year-Old Linux Bug, Raises AI Coding Questions
ai.body:
A developer using Claude Code uncovered a Linux kernel vulnerability that had sat undetected for 23 years - a striking demonstration of what AI-assisted code review can surface that human eyes miss. The find is generating serious discussion about whether AI tools should become standard in security audits.

Separately, a new paper shows that self-distillation, a surprisingly simple technique where a model learns from its own outputs, meaningfully improves code generation quality. And researchers have published a breakdown of how emotion-like concepts function inside large language models, adding to a growing body of interpretability work. Anthropic contributed to that last study directly, continuing its push to understand what its models are actually doing internally.

sources.title: Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years
Embarrassingly simple self-distillation improves code generation
Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Components of a Coding Agent

ai.headline: Iranian Missiles Hit AWS Data Centers in Bahrain and Dubai
ai.body:
Iranian missile strikes destroyed AWS data centers in Bahrain and Dubai, forcing Amazon to declare hard-down status across multiple availability zones. The attack marks one of the most significant physical strikes on cloud infrastructure on record, with cascading outages hitting customers across the region.

The incident exposes a long-discussed but rarely tested vulnerability: hyperscale cloud providers concentrate enormous economic and operational power in a small number of physical buildings. Redundancy across zones is designed to handle hardware failures and natural disasters. A coordinated military strike is a different threat model entirely.

sources.title: Iranian missile blitz takes down AWS data centers in Bahrain and Dubai

ai.headline: AI Fakes and Copyright Trolls Are Targeting Folk Musicians
ai.body:
A folk musician found herself caught between two emerging threats - AI-generated fakes of her voice and a copyright troll exploiting the confusion around AI-produced content. The case illustrates how independent artists face a pincer movement: synthetic copies erode their audience while bad-faith legal claims drain their resources.

Elsewhere, the question of proving human authorship is becoming a real market problem. Creators are now seeking logos and certifications to signal their work is AI-free, a trend The Verge documents as clients increasingly demand proof. Microsoft is not helping clarity - one analysis counted a sprawling, overlapping family of products all named some variant of Copilot, making it harder for anyone to track what AI tools they are actually using.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it
How many products does Microsoft have named 'Copilot'?

btc.headline: Bitcoin Draws Institutional Backing as Market Liquidity Thins
btc.body:
A former UK Chancellor has publicly endorsed Bitcoin as a viable alternative to failing financial systems, adding political weight to a growing institutional narrative. Meanwhile, Mercado Bitcoin's research shows Bitcoin consistently outperforms gold and equities in the aftermath of global shocks, reinforcing its case as a crisis hedge.

Beneath the bullish framing, however, the market structure looks fragile. Five separate data sources point to thinning liquidity inside the Bitcoin order book, a warning sign that large moves could be amplified in either direction. Digital asset treasuries are also under pressure to generate real yield rather than simply hold coins, signaling that passive accumulation strategies are losing credibility with investors.

sources.title: Bitcoin tends to outperform gold and stocks after global shocks, Mercado Bitcoin finds
Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems
Digital asset treasuries must now earn their keep
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside

btc.headline: Bears Sitting on $2.5B Risk as Bitcoin Eyes $72K
btc.body:
Short sellers are dangerously exposed. Data shows roughly $2.5 billion in Bitcoin short positions face liquidation if the price pushes to $72,000, a level that now looks within reach given current momentum.

The squeeze risk comes at a tense moment for market structure. Liquidity is thinning, meaning a relatively small surge in buying pressure could trigger a cascade of forced short closures and accelerate the move sharply upward. Bears who built positions expecting continued weakness may find the trade reversing fast.

sources.title: Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside

btc.headline: Prediction Markets Face Legal Walls Across Multiple Jurisdictions
btc.body:
Prediction markets are running into regulators on several fronts simultaneously. A Nevada judge extended a ban on Kalshi, rejecting the platform's argument that its event contracts fall outside gambling law. Polymarket separately pulled a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash, highlighting the reputational risks alongside the legal ones.

The pressure is not limited to the US. Platforms testing Asian markets are encountering strict local frameworks that treat prediction contracts as unlicensed gambling by default. The sector is expanding fast, but the legal architecture in most jurisdictions has not moved with it.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense
Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets
Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash

btc.headline: Solana Faces Quantum Security Tradeoff; Bitcoin Debates Node Philosophy
btc.body:
Solana's engineers are grappling with a fundamental tension: adding quantum-resistant cryptography meaningfully slows the network, undermining the speed that defines its competitive position. The tradeoff has no clean answer, and the timeline for quantum threats remains uncertain, making the cost-benefit calculation harder.

On the Bitcoin side, developer Jimmy Song is pushing for a more conservative node client, arguing that Bitcoin's resilience depends on software that prioritizes stability and predictability over new features. The debate reflects a broader tension in open-source blockchain development between caution and progress.

sources.title: Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client
Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-caramelised leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that balances umami with subtle sweetness.
food.tip: Rinse sliced leeks in cold water and dry them thoroughly before caramelising — residual moisture turns them soggy instead of golden.

exercise.name: The Deck of Cards Workout
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards, exercise mat
exercise.description: Assign a bodyweight movement to each suit, flip cards one by one, and perform the number of reps shown — the randomness keeps your nervous system guessing and makes the session fly by.

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to humans, whose cells, beliefs, and memories constantly change, it becomes a genuinely unsettling question about what 'you' actually are.
learn.why: On a quiet Saturday it's the perfect rabbit hole: it connects Greek philosophy, Derek Parfit's modern work on personal identity, and even debates in neuroscience about the continuity of consciousness — one hour in and you'll question whether the person who wakes up tomorrow is really you.
